problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem A (continuous): build a model for how fungi decompose woody fibers/ground litter, where fungi differ in growth rate and moisture tolerance. Required: capture inter-fungal interactions, short- and long-term trends, sensitivity to rapid environmental fluctuations, the effect of broader atmospheric/climate trends, predictions of which species and species-combinations persist across arid/semi-arid/temperate/arboreal/tropical biomes, and the role of biodiversity in decomposition efficiency. This O-award paper (team 2110178) narrows the universe to three concrete fungi — white-rot (WR), brown-rot (BR), and Aspergillus niger (AN) — and assigns each a substrate (WR/BR decompose lignin, AN decomposes cellulose) to make the system tractable.
models_used: Model I - Multi-Fungi Decomposition Model: enzyme-catalyzed reaction kinetics (A + E0 -> E0A -> E0 + P) modeled with the Michaelis-Menten equation r_c = V_E*E*C/(K_m + C), coupled to a first-order enzyme-inactivation ODE dE/dt = -k_d*E, giving a 2-ODE system for enzyme concentration E and substrate concentration C, solved analytically to E = aP0*exp(-k_d*t) and a closed-form expression for decomposition time t(C)

Competitiveness Index I_c: re-interpretation of the enzyme inactivation coefficient k_d as a fungus-specific competitiveness index, I_c = f(u, m) where u = hyphal extension/growth rate and m = moisture tolerance index; reduced to a linear law I_c = 0.00176*u after OLS fitting, yielding I_c values WR=0.015, BR=0.009, AN=0.021
OLS linear regression to fit moisture-tolerance index vs decomposition-rate index (and growth-rate vs moisture-tolerance m = g(u))
Model II short-term: single-species Logistic growth dx/dt = r*x*(1 - x/n) over a 7-day horizon (resources abundant, no competition)

Model II long-term: two-species Lotka-Volterra competition system dx/dt = r1*x*(1 - x/n1 - s1*y/n2), dy/dt = r2*y*(1 - y/n2 - s2*x/n2) over 500 days, with competition coefficient s switched between regimes (s1=s2=2 for exclusion, s1=s2=0.5 for coexistence) to encode whether pairs compete for the same substrate (WR&BR) or partition resources (WR&AN, BR&AN)

Model III environment coupling: piecewise-linear growth-rate-vs-temperature law (linear rise to species-specific optimum, abrupt death above max temp) and a humidity -> moisture-tolerance -> growth-rate chain; environment feeds growth rate u, which feeds I_c, which feeds decomposition rate D (chains T->u->Ic->D and H->m->u->Ic->D)
Sensitivity analysis: sweeping temperature (10/20/30 C bands) and humidity, re-injecting the resulting growth rates into the Lotka-Volterra model to observe shifts in dominance/coexistence

Scenario/prediction layer: mapping real biomes to representative (T,H) values (Washington temperate ~7/23 C, Gansu semi-arid 20-60%, Sahara arid <=20%, Congo tropical >90%/25-30 C) and tabulating decomposition rates to rank advantaged vs disadvantaged species and pairs
key_techniques: Decoupling the problem into a clean 3-layer cascade: chemistry/decomposition (M-M kinetics) -> population dynamics (Logistic/Lotka-Volterra) -> environment (T,H sensitivity), so each model's output is the next model's input
Reducing an abstract, open-ended biology prompt to three named, parameterized fungi with a one-substrate-per-fungus assumption, making every later step concrete and computable

Bridging chemistry and ecology by re-casting the physically-motivated enzyme inactivation constant k_d as the ecological competitiveness index I_c = f(u,m) — a single parameter that carries both growth rate and moisture tolerance, the two traits the prompt explicitly called out
Time-scale separation: choosing Logistic for 7 days (linear, resource-rich) and Lotka-Volterra for 500 days (competition binds) to directly answer the 'short-term vs long-term' sub-question
Encoding qualitative ecological outcomes (competitive exclusion vs coexistence) as discrete competition-coefficient regimes (s=2 vs s=0.5) tied to whether two fungi share a substrate
Piecewise-linear trait-response curves (growth rate vs temperature with a hard cutoff at max temp) as a deliberately simple but defensible functional form
Feeding environmental sensitivity results back into the population model rather than analyzing them in isolation, so climate effects propagate through interactions to dominance predictions
Grounding the biome predictions in real geographic exemplars with cited representative T/H values, turning a hand-wave into a concrete results table
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on coherence and answer-completeness, not on mathematical depth. It hits every bullet of the prompt — multi-fungi interaction, short/long term, rapid-fluctuation sensitivity, atmospheric-trend impact, biome-by-biome persistence predictions, and biodiversity — in a clearly signposted order, so a triage judge can check every box fast. Its decisive move is the I_c = f(u,m) competitiveness index: it manufactures a single, interpretable coupling parameter that ties the chemistry layer to the ecology layer AND directly operationalizes the exact two traits (growth rate, moisture tolerance) the problem named. The Logistic-then-Lotka-Volterra time-scale split is an elegant, textbook-grounded answer to 'short- vs long-term' that reads as principled rather than ad hoc. The competition-coefficient regimes produce crisp, narratable conclusions (WR excludes BR; AN coexists with both and is synergistic) that map onto believable biology. Finally it closes the loop: environmental sensitivity is pushed back through the interaction model into concrete, tabulated biome predictions, and it delivers the required non-technical 'article' for a general audience. The whole thing is internally consistent and tells one clean story end to end — exactly what survives the multi-pass judging funnel.
section_highlights: Summary block states all three models and their headline findings up front (synergy WR&AN, inhibition WR&BR, coexistence outcomes) — a judge knows the conclusions before reading the body. The 'Our Work' section plus a flowchart (Figure 2) gives an at-a-glance model architecture. Assumptions are explicitly justified and, notably, each is promised to be 're-stated once used' — disciplined assumption hygiene. The Notations table standardizes symbols early. Each model section follows a consistent build -> derive -> result -> analysis rhythm, and Model III uses two small flowcharts (Figures 10 and 12) to make the T->u->Ic->D and H->m->u->Ic->D causal chains explicit. Results are delivered as decision-ready tables (Figures 16 and 17) listing advantaged/disadvantaged species per biome. The Strengths/Weaknesses section is honest and specific. Section 9 'An Article' fulfills the deliverable of communicating to a lay audience.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The competitiveness equation I_c = 0.00176*u (Eq. 8) is dimensionally and numerically murky and inconsistent with the tabulated I_c values (e.g. growth rates 0.05/0.025/0.075 do not map to 0.015/0.009/0.021 under that single coefficient), and m drops out despite I_c = f(u,m) being claimed — the headline coupling parameter is under-derived

Parameters are largely taken from scattered literature look-ups with no real calibration or uncertainty quantification; the authors themselves flag the moisture-tolerance OLS fit as weak due to scarce data (only ~7 scatter points)

The one-fungus-one-substrate assumption (WR/BR only lignin, AN only cellulose) is biologically crude — real WR/BR degrade cellulose too — and the cellulose-lignin 'entanglement synergy' between AN and the rot fungi is asserted narratively rather than modeled mechanistically
The s = 2 vs s = 0.5 competition coefficients are hand-set to force exclusion vs coexistence rather than estimated from data, so the long-term outcomes are partly assumed into existence
Piecewise-linear growth-vs-temperature with instantaneous death above T_max is a sharp idealization; no smooth optimum curve, no humidity-temperature interaction except in narrative

Only pairwise fungal combinations are analyzed (authors acknowledge 3+ species may introduce error); the biodiversity section (7) is purely qualitative with no quantitative diversity-vs-efficiency metric

Several figures appear to show schematic/illustrative rather than simulation-faithful data (e.g. multiple humidity curves overlapping identically in Figure 13), and the carbon-cycle diagram in the article section is degraded/garbled
Hemicellulose is named in assumptions but effectively dropped from the modeling, a stated weakness
reusable_patterns: The 3-layer cascade template for any 'agents interacting in an environment' problem: micro-mechanism model -> population/interaction dynamics -> environmental modulation, wired so each layer outputs the next layer's parameter
Manufacture a single interpretable coupling index from the traits the prompt explicitly names (here I_c from growth rate + moisture tolerance) to bridge two otherwise-separate sub-models
Answer 'short-term vs long-term' by switching the governing equation with the time scale (Logistic when a constraint is slack, Lotka-Volterra/full competition when it binds)
Encode qualitative regime outcomes (exclusion vs coexistence) as a small set of discrete parameter settings (s=2 vs s=0.5) keyed to a structural condition (shared vs partitioned resource)

Do sensitivity analysis by re-injecting perturbed parameters back through the downstream model rather than reporting local derivatives in isolation, so effects propagate to the final decision variable
Turn open-ended scenario questions into a results table by mapping each scenario to a real-world exemplar with cited representative parameter values, then ranking outcomes

State the headline conclusions in the Summary, give a model-architecture flowchart in the intro, and add small causal-chain flowcharts before each derivation so judges can follow the logic at a glance
Include an explicit, specific Strengths/Weaknesses section and (when required) a separate plain-language 'article' deliverable to maximize box-ticking against the prompt
Discipline of justifying every assumption and re-stating it at point of use
